findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a focal mass located within the spinal canal that displaces the spinal cord and expands the subarachnoid space, placing it in the intradural extramedullary compartment. The lesion is surrounded by cerebrospinal fluid and typically demonstrates intense, homogeneous enhancement following contrast administration.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the presence of nodular, 'sugar coating' enhancement along the surface of the spinal cord and meninges, suggestive of disseminated leptomeningeal spread. Additional similar enhancing nodules of varied sizes may be seen scattered throughout the subarachnoid space.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of expansion of the spinal cord itself, which would suggest an intramedullary lesion. Furthermore, there is no adjacent bony remodelling, scalloping, or widening of the neural exit foramina to suggest a slow-growing benign nerve sheath tumor.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an intradural extramedullary enhancing mass with associated leptomeningeal nodularity, strongly suggesting cerebrospinal fluid dissemination.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Drop metastasis (astrocytoma), given the intradural extramedullary location combined with evidence of leptomeningeal seeding. Drop metastases result from the dissemination of a primary central nervous system tumor, such as an astrocytoma, via the cerebrospinal fluid pathways.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Nerve sheath tumor, however these are typically solitary lesions that frequently extend through and widen the neural foramina, creating a classic 'dumbbell' appearance. Furthermore, nerve sheath tumors like schwannomas are slow-growing and commonly cause adjacent bony remodelling, which is absent in this case.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Meningioma, given it is the second most common intradural extramedullary mass and missing it could delay potentially curative surgical resection. However, meningiomas classically have a broad-based dural attachment with a dural tail and often contain calcification, rather than presenting with diffuse drop metastases.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate the entire neuroaxis to identify the primary intracranial tumor and map the full extent of leptomeningeal dissemination.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to establish whether the patient has a known history of a primary central nervous system malignancy, such as a cranial astrocytoma or medulloblastoma.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire brain and whole spine, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely identifying the primary intracranial neoplasm and detecting further occult drop metastases throughout the cerebrospinal fluid spaces.
management.mriSequences: I would specify sagittal and axial pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to thoroughly screen the neuroaxis for enhancing meningeal 'sugar coating' and nodular drop metastases, alongside T2-weighted sequences to assess for any associated cord compression.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact levels of all intradural extramedullary deposits, the presence and location of the suspected primary cranial tumor, and any evidence of spinal cord compression to guide complex neuro-oncological management.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a focal mass located within the spinal canal that displaces the spinal cord and expands the subarachnoid space, placing it in the intradural extramedullary compartment. The lesion is surrounded by cerebrospinal fluid and typically demonstrates intense, homogeneous enhancement following contrast administration. Supporting features include the presence of nodular, 'sugar coating' enhancement along the surface of the spinal cord and meninges, suggestive of disseminated leptomeningeal spread. Additional similar enhancing nodules of varied sizes may be seen scattered throughout the subarachnoid space. Importantly, there is no evidence of expansion of the spinal cord itself, which would suggest an intramedullary lesion. Furthermore, there is no adjacent bony remodelling, scalloping, or widening of the neural exit foramina to suggest a slow-growing benign nerve sheath tumor. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an intradural extramedullary enhancing mass with associated leptomeningeal nodularity, strongly suggesting cerebrospinal fluid dissemination.

The appearances are most in keeping with Drop metastasis (astrocytoma), given the intradural extramedullary location combined with evidence of leptomeningeal seeding. Drop metastases result from the dissemination of a primary central nervous system tumor, such as an astrocytoma, via the cerebrospinal fluid pathways. The principle differential would be Nerve sheath tumor, however these are typically solitary lesions that frequently extend through and widen the neural foramina, creating a classic 'dumbbell' appearance. Furthermore, nerve sheath tumors like schwannomas are slow-growing and commonly cause adjacent bony remodelling, which is absent in this case. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Meningioma, given it is the second most common intradural extramedullary mass and missing it could delay potentially curative surgical resection. However, meningiomas classically have a broad-based dural attachment with a dural tail and often contain calcification, rather than presenting with diffuse drop metastases.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate the entire neuroaxis to identify the primary intracranial tumor and map the full extent of leptomeningeal dissemination. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to establish whether the patient has a known history of a primary central nervous system malignancy, such as a cranial astrocytoma or medulloblastoma. I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire brain and whole spine, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely identifying the primary intracranial neoplasm and detecting further occult drop metastases throughout the cerebrospinal fluid spaces. I would specify sagittal and axial pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to thoroughly screen the neuroaxis for enhancing meningeal 'sugar coating' and nodular drop metastases, alongside T2-weighted sequences to assess for any associated cord compression. This should go to the Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact levels of all intradural extramedullary deposits, the presence and location of the suspected primary cranial tumor, and any evidence of spinal cord compression to guide complex neuro-oncological management.
